game_name: Ogre Battle: The March of the Black Queen

design_philosophy.core_loop: Deploy squads to liberate towns/temples, manage alignment reputation, progress through branching stages with real-time tactical deployment and turn-based battles
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Moral choice between lawful good and chaotic evil conduct, with permanent consequences for ending and character recruitment
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Lead a revolutionary band against an oppressive empire, making strategic decisions about military deployment and ethical conduct
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: The game rigidly enforces moral alignment consequences—player choices during liberation (troop selection, city defense priorities, accepting/rejecting moral missions) directly lock characters out and determine which of 13 endings is accessible. This is not cosmetic; it gates story progression and character availability. The tarot-based character creation system that determines starting abilities based on moral philosophy responses further commits players to a specific archetype from the start.

difficulty.lives_system: Unit-based retreat system; units can be defeated and retreated from combat without permanent loss, but squad composition affects battle outcomes
difficulty.continues_system: Player can retry individual stages after completion; already-liberated enemy units do not respawn, reducing escalation pressure on replays
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based checkpointing; world map allows stage selection and replay, with persistent liberation state across attempts
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency: player controls squad composition, unit deployment timing, tarot card usage, and tactical withdrawal. Difficulty is primarily a function of unit organization and strategic positioning rather than reflexes.

progression.upgrade_system: Class advancement system allows characters to change classes and develop abilities. Equipment purchases from liberated town shops. Tarot card collection provides tactical flexibility during battles.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Story progression unlocks new stages sequentially, but player can replay completed stages. Hidden stages accessible through main game progression; one easter egg stage. Character recruitment gated by reputation alignment thresholds.
replayability_hooks: 13 different endings based on conduct and reputation management
Tarot-based character creation system creates different starting configurations
Alignment system creates branching character availability
Hidden stages and treasure caches encourage stage replay
Neutral monsters respawn on completed stages for resource grinding

world_building_techniques: Tarot card divination framing device for character creation and town liberation outcomes
NPC dialogue in liberated towns provides lore and mission context
Moral reputation system creates player-driven narrative divergence
Character alignment mechanics embed ethical storytelling into gameplay systems
Stage-based world exploration revealing hidden locations and lore

boss_design.philosophy: Each of 25 main stages concludes with a boss encounter that functions within the same tactical squad-based system as regular battles. Boss encounters are narrative endpoints for stage objectives rather than isolated combat challenges.

faction_indicators: Revolution reputation score (lawful good vs chaotic evil spectrum)
Choice of moral troops for town liberation
danger_signals: Low revolution reputation locks out high-alignment character recruitment
High reputation locks out low-alignment character recruitment
Allowing liberated cities to be reclaimed damages reputation
Specific ending thresholds inaccessible to players below/above reputation boundaries
environmental_storytelling: Liberated towns and temples persist on map, showing player's territorial control
Tarot card outcomes on liberation create localized narrative variation
Day/night cycle affects enemy unit types (vampires, werewolves) and morale mechanics
Maintenance costs for deployed squads create visible economic pressure

sources_used: wikipedia - Ogre Battle: The March of the Black Queen (gameplay, alignment, battles, narrative, endings, development)
